original_latex:
Let $t\geq 1$ and let $d_t$ be the density of the set of integers $n\in\mathbb{N}$ for which $t$ can be represented as the sum of distinct divisors of $n$.

Do there exist constants $c_1,c_2>0$ such that\[d_t \sim \frac{c_1}{(\log t)^{c_2}}\]as $t\to \infty$?
additional_context:
ErdŇĎs \cite{Er70} proved that d_t always exists, and that there exist some constants c_3,c_4>0 such that(1)/((\log t)^{c_3)} < d_t < (1)/((\log t)^{c_4)}.

References

[Er70] ErdŇĎs, Paul, Some extremal problems in combinatorial number theory. Mathematical Essays Dedicated to A. J. Macintyre (1970), 123-133.
reference_proof_hint:
As far as I can find in the literature, this is **open**.

Let
[
A_t:={n\in\mathbb N:\ t\text{ is a sum of distinct divisors of }n},
\qquad
d_t:=\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{|A_t\cap[1,x]|}{x}.
]
ErdŇĎs proved that **the natural density (d_t) exists for each fixed $t$** [[nomath]](in particular, $A_t$ is ‚Äúeventually periodic‚ÄĚ in a strong sense: every $n\in A_t$ is a multiple of some element of $A_t$ not exceeding $t!$)[[/nomath]]. ([Scribd][1])

He also proved that (d_t\to 0) and gave explicit **logarithmic power bounds**. In the notation of his paper, after splitting (A_t) into two classes and estimating each, he obtains an upper bound of order (1/\log t) and a lower bound of order (1/(\log t)^2): for all sufficiently large $t$,
[
d_t \ll \frac{1}{\log t}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
d_t \gg \frac{1}{(\log t)^2}.
]
More concretely, he states that the relevant class has density (\le 2/\log t), hence (d_t\to 0) and ‚Äúin fact (d_t<1/(\log t)^1)‚ÄĚ for $t$ large, and also that one can prove (d_t>1/(\log

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# ErdŇĎs Problem 859

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/859](https://www.erdosproblems.com/859)
-/

namespace Erdos859

/--
`DivisorSumSet t` is the set of natural numbers `n` such that `t` can be represented as
a sum of distinct divisors of `n`.
-/
def DivisorSumSet (t : ‚Ąē) := { n : ‚Ąē | ‚ąÉ s ‚äÜ Nat.divisors n, t = ‚ąĎ i ‚ąą s, i }

open Asymptotics Filter

/-- A weaker version of the problem proved by Erdos:
The density `d‚āú` of `DivisorSumSet (t : ‚Ąē)` is bounded from below by `1 / log (t) ^ c‚āÉ` and
from above by `1 / log (t) ^ c‚āĄ` for some positive constants `c‚āÉ` and `c‚āĄ`.
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_859.variants.erdos_upper_lower_bounds : ‚ąÉŠĶČ (c‚āÉ > (0 : ‚ĄĚ)) (c‚āĄ > (0 : ‚ĄĚ)) (t‚āÄ : ‚Ąē),
    ‚ąÄŠ∂† t in atTop, ‚ąÉ d‚āú : ‚ĄĚ, (DivisorSumSet t).HasDensity d‚āú ‚ąß
    1 / Real.log t ^ c‚āÉ < d‚āú ‚ąß d‚āú < 1 / Real.log t ^ c‚āĄ := by
  sorry


/-
**ErdŇĎs Problem 859**
The density `d‚āú` of `DivisorSumSet (t : ‚Ąē)` is assymptotically equivalent to ` c‚āĀ / log (t) ^ c‚āā`
for some positive constants `c‚āĀ` and `c‚āā`.
-/
@[category research open, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_859 :
    ‚ąÉ c‚āĀ > 0, ‚ąÉ c‚āā > (0 : ‚ĄĚ), ‚ąÉ d : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ, (‚ąÄ t > 0, (DivisorSumSet t).HasDensity (d t)) ‚ąß
      (fun (t : ‚Ąē) ‚Ü¶ d t) ~[atTop] (fun t ‚Ü¶ c‚āĀ / Real.log t ^ c‚āā) := by
  sorry

/-
A case where we can easily calculate the density of `DivisorSumSet t` is that of `t=0`.
-/
@[category high_school, AMS 11]
lemma erdos_859.variants.trivial_case : DivisorSumSet 0 = Set.univ := by
  simp [DivisorSumSet, Exists.intro ‚ąÖ]

/-
An easy sanity check is to prove that for every natural number `t` the density `d‚āú` is
a positive number.
Hint: investigate some multiplicative structure of `DivisorSumSet t`.
-/
@[category undergraduate, AMS 11]
lemma erdos_859.variants.positive_density (t : ‚Ąē) :
    (DivisorSumSet t).HasPosDensity := by
  sorry

end Erdos859
